title: "Obsidian Mirror Problem v10007"

  description: "At 21:35, the Obsidian Mirror vanished from its glass case in the south archives. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) were recorded via access logs near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements, while the other three speak only truths. Only one suspect is guilty. Each suspect issues five declarations:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'If 2 is lying, then 4 was definitely not near the case.'
      (2) 'If 3 is telling the truth, then 2’s timeline must be completely false.'
      (3) 'I left the archives at 21:25, so I could not have stolen the Mirror at 21:35.'
      (4) '4 was indeed at the case around 21:30, contradicting their denial.'
      (5) 'Either 2 never lingered past 21:20, or 3 is wrong in every statement—but never both.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 actually stayed until 21:40, contradicting their 21:25 claim.'
      (2) 'I never went near that Mirror after 21:10, so I am no thief.'
      (3) 'If 3 is lying, then 4 must be fully honest.'
      (4) 'If 1 is truthful, then 3 is lying in all statements.'
      (5) 'Either 4 is not guilty, or 1’s timeline is totally wrong—but not both at once.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I did not approach the Mirror after 21:05.'
      (2) 'If 4 is telling the truth, then 2’s claim (2) is entirely false.'
      (3) 'Either 2 is lying, or 1’s 21:25 departure is incorrect—but they can’t both be true.'
      (4) 'I am not guilty; logs show me in another corridor around 21:30.'
      (5) '1 was definitely at the case at 21:30, which conflicts with the 21:25 story.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 was at the Mirror around 21:30, contradicting that 21:05 denial.'
      (2) 'If 1 is lying, then 2’s timeline is correct in every detail.'
      (3) 'I am not guilty; I was on a different floor at 21:35.'
      (4) 'Either 2 hovered near the case after 21:10, or 3’s statements are completely false—but not both.'
      (5) 'If 3 is truthful, then 1’s departure at 21:25 cannot be right.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Obsidian Mirror? Provide only a single digit."

answer: >
  explanation: "Formal Logic:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all five statements,' T(x) = 'x is entirely truthful,' G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one L(x)=true, one G(x)=true, others T(x)=true.

  - Case L(1):
    1(1)–(5) false => times or blame leads to contradictions with 3(5) vs 1(3). Fails.

  - Case L(2):
    2(1)–(5) false => 2 was near the Mirror after 21:10 => suspicious. Meanwhile 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(3) 'Left 21:25 => not culprit,' 3(4) 'I am not guilty => logs corridor,' 4(3) 'I am not guilty => different floor' => G(2)=true. No contradiction.

  - Case L(3):
    3(1)–(5) false => 3 approached after 21:05 => suspicious. 1,2,4 all truthful => contradictions about who was actually present at 21:30. Fails.

  - Case L(4):
    4(1)–(5) false => 1,2,3 are truthful => times or blame produce mismatch (like 3(5) vs 1(3)). Contradiction.

  Only L(2)=>G(2) remains consistent: suspect 2 is the thief.

  final: '2'"
